data.title: GE Vernova: A Blowout Q1, Raised Guidance, And Why I'm Hiking My Target
data.description:
GE Vernova Inc. (GEV) soared 8% in the premarket on Wednesday, April 22, following its Q1 2026 earnings report. The double-beat-and-raise was welcome news to AI investors and the market writ large.

I was bullish on GEV back in early March. While that was not even two months ago, the stock is up 27% since my previous analysis, blowing past even my optimistic price objective then. Today, with new numbers in hand, I am raising my fundamental price target and sticking with a buy rating. I’ll also provide a fresh look at the technicals.

GEV +217% YoY Coming Into The Q1 Print

In April, GEV reported a stunning set of quarterly results. Q1 GAAP EPS of $17.44 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast by $15.44, while revenue of $9.3 billion, up more than 16% from the same period a year earlier, was a $90 million beat. The company hiked its revenue guidance slightly compared to what was served up in January, but its free cash flow forecast increased markedly.

Shares soared 8% later in the morning after the numbers hit the tape. If it holds to the close, the post-earnings gain would be the best since last July. The options market had implied just a 5.2% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after Wednesday’s release. Implied volatility of 55% should retreat toward 40% if history is a guide. Short interest on the stock, meanwhile, is low at 2.7%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, GE Vernova delivered a record-breaking first quarter in 2026, highlighted by a massive beat-and-raise driven by surging demand for electrification and data center infrastructure. The company reported 7% organic top-line growth and a big increase in net income to $4.75 billion. To be clear, the bottom line was significantly boosted by a $3.99 billion pre-tax gain from the Prolec GE acquisition.

Color On Quarter

But big picture, profitability was robust across the portfolio, with adjusted EBITDA tagging $896 million, a 14% beat vs. consensus, and margins expanding by 390 basis points to 9.6%. Maybe most impressive, the company generated $4.8 billion in free cash flow during the quarter, already surpassing its total free cash flow for the entire year of 2025, largely due to over $5 billion in equipment down payments.

Operational momentum was strong, too, with organic orders skyrocketing 71% to $18.3 billion, resulting in a book-to-bill ratio of 1.96x. According to the Q1 presentation, the growth was led by the Electrification and Power segments, which saw organic order increases of 86% and 59%, respectively, as the company secured $2.4 billion in data center equipment orders and reached 100 GW in gas power equipment contracts ahead of schedule.

Elsewhere, GEV’s Wind segment reported an EBITDA loss of $382 million, but it showed signs of recovery with an 85% organic increase in orders over the January through March period.

New 2026 Guidance

The street was blown away by GEV’s record $163 billion backlog, along with management raising its full-year 2026 guidance, increasing the revenue target range to $44.5–$45.5 billion, and hiking aforementioned free cash flow numbers to $6.5–$7.5 billion.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is likely to be revised sharply higher by the sell-side in the days ahead due to the massive GAAP per-share earnings figure just reported. As it stands, there have been a high 17 Wall Street EPS upgrades in the past 90 days, compared to just 4 downgrades. Indeed, profitability trends are sublime, even coming into the Q1 print, as GEV earned an A+ Factor Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system.

GE Vernova: Revenue & Earnings Outlook, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates to my previous numbers. I’m focusing on the free cash flow guidance hike to guide the price target. The increase was a bit more than 30%.

So, if we multiply the $22.54 figure from last time by 30%, we arrive at potentially $29 of normalized forward non-GAAP EPS. Pair that with the same 40x P/E I used last time, and we arrive at an $1160 target. So, that leaves 9% of upside from here.

GEV: Premium Valuation Metrics, Major EPS & FCF Growth

Key risks for GEV include weaker AI demand, namely the government halting offshore wind projects. Of course, competition remains stout in the electrification space, and up-and-coming companies could slowly eat into GEV’s future agreements. As GEV’s backlog grows, the onus is on the management team to execute its long-term strategy in this high-growth period.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a projected Q2 2026 earnings date of Wednesday, July 29, for BMO. The company hosts its Annual General Meeting on Wednesday, May 20.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With the stock up by more than 200% YoY, GEV’s technical situation is among the most powerful in the market today. Notice in the chart below that shares remain in a protracted uptrend, which dates back to last November. A series of higher highs and higher lows in price have come alongside an RSI momentum oscillator that ranges between 40 and 80, which is also what the bulls like to see.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. There are now two lingering gaps. The first is from the post-earnings reaction at $991, while the second is further down at $915. Those bear watching for the possibility that they could be filled during pullbacks.

Of course, GEV is now quite extended by most technical indicators, including the RSI and distance from its 200dma.

GEV: Bullish Uptrend, Strong RSI & Rising 200dma, Mind The Gaps

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on GEV. Shares are up fantastically in 2026, and today’s strong post-earnings rally is backed up by significantly higher free cash flow guidance, which I expect to translate into EPS. The stock is overbought, but I see it more as a ‘buy the dip’ technical situation.

data.title: Star Bulk Carriers: Updated Dividend Policy And Macro Tailwinds Triggered A Buy (Upgrade)
data.description:
I previously reiterated my Hold rating for Star Bulk Carriers Corp. (SBLK) in December 2025, given the notably lower dividend yields.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am upgrading SBLK as a Buy, thanks to the macro tailwinds and the updated dividend policy.

SBLK Enjoys New Tailwinds Entering FY2026

For now, the bulls have proven my Hold thesis wrong indeed, with SBLK enjoying a double digits rally to retest the prior pandemic stock price highs of $24s at the time of writing.

Post pandemic consolidation, as:

  • SBLK merges with Eagle Bulk Shipping Inc in 2024,
  • CMB.Tech (NYSE:CMBT) acquires Golden Ocean (NASDAQ:GOGL) in 2025,
  • Diana Shipping (DSX) attempts to takeover Genco Shipping (GNK) in 2026, and
  • Hapag-Lloyd (HPGLY) (HLAGF) acquires ZIM Integrated Shipping (ZIM) in 2026 (container sector),

it is apparent that some of their capacity expansion efforts are paying off, as the shipping market enters another turbulent and likely to be highly profitable 2026 year.

1. Rising Spot Prices

As a result of:

  • the "Red Sea transit" risks and the "higher costs" linked to the Cape of Good Hope route, along with
  • the Iran conflict and the impacted Hormuz passage,

the Baltic Exchange Dry Index has risen to $2,567 as of April 17, 2026, against the bottom of $735 observed in January 31, 2025 and the average of ~$1,200 in 2019.

The same has been observed in the notably higher spot prices for:

  • Panamax/Kamsarmax at an average of $20K for short charter, and
  • Supramax/Ultramax at an average of $18K as of April 15, 2026,

compared to SBLK's:

  • FQ1'26 contracted rates guidance of $15.39K, page 14, (+51% YoY) and $15.57K (+47% YoY), along with
  • FY2025 rates at $12.59K and $13.14K,
  • FY2019 rates at $11.18K and $10.17K, respectively.

These two vessel types are important indeed, since they comprise 115 units or the equivalent 81.5% of SBLK's overall fleet capacity as of FQ4'25, on a fully delivered basis.

I am of the opinion that the management may guide the higher contracted TCE rates for FQ2'26 in the upcoming FQ1'26 earnings call on May 20, 2026 (Post-Market), with it securing their robust, profitable growth prospects in H1'26, if not through 2026, pending a resolution in the Middle East.

These reasons may also explain why SBLK has been well buoyed by the rising tide, as similarly observed in its dry bulk peers in varying degrees.

2. Richer Performance Metrics

The same has been embedded in SBLK's performance metrics in FQ4'25, with the higher Daily Time Charter Equivalent Rate [TCE] of $19.01K (+17.9% YoY) already well balancing the lower fleets at 137 (-10.1% YoY) to deliver the stable revenue performance at $300.58M (-2.6% YoY).

Combined with the relatively low average daily OPEX at $5,045 (inline YoY) against the peer group averages at $5,640, it is unsurprising that the company has been able to report the richer adj EBITDA at $126.42M (+21.7% YoY) and margins at 42% (+8.4 points YoY/+13.2 from FY2019 levels of 28.8%).

These numbers lend further credence to SBLK's ongoing cost optimization and fleet renewal cadence, as they "divesting older, less efficient vessels and upgrading the remaining fleet with energy-saving devices, shaft power limitation, propeller silicone coatings, propeller trimming, and the commissioning of onboard telemetry systems."

This is especially since newer fleets typically also command a TCE premium, attributed to the "high specifications and low fuel consumption."

3. Updated Dividend Policy

While I have previously lamented SBLK's amended dividend policy in late 2024, where the management cuts the dividends paid out from the prior policy of 100% to "60% of cash flow from operations less debt amortization, less maintenance/upgrade CAPEX less any deficit of cash below $2.1 million per owned vessel (“Cash Flow”), towards quarterly shareholder dividends," with the balance allocated across share repurchases and vessel growth opportunities, things are no longer the same.

This is because SBLK has updated their shareholder return policy in the recent FQ4'25 earnings call, to "distribute 100% of cash flow from operations, after debt service, maintenance/upgrade Capex and any deficit of cash below $2.1 million per owned vessel to the shareholders. A minimum dividend of $0.05 per share per quarter will remain in place."

With FQ4'25 already bringing forth $0.37 in dividend payout per share (+236% QoQ/+311.1% YoY), it is unsurprising that SBLK has also hit a new 52 week high of $27s post FQ4'25 earnings call, as the market and investors celebrate the richer dividend policy.

While it remains to be seen how the rest of 2026 may develop, readers may want to note that their ongoing fleet renewal already contributes to the relatively younger fleet age at 12.1 years, compared to the sector's "average fleet age of roughly 15 years."

Combined with the relatively low orderbook at "only around 8%–12% of the fleet" and the consequent "supply-side scarcity," I am of the opinion that the wider dry bulk sector, SBLK included, may enjoy a robust pricing power in the intermediate term.

This is especially since the management has authorized a $100M share repurchase program, with it building upon the -3.6% of its float retired in FY2025, albeit with a distance remaining between its current shares outstanding of 113.97M in FQ4'25 and the pre-merger sum of 84.17M in FQ1'24.

SBLK Is Upgraded As A Buy

As a result of the numerous tailwinds from macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainties along with the new dividend policy, it is unsurprising that SBLK has also been well defended along the uptrend momentum, with the stock retesting the prior 2021-2024 highs at the time of writing.

Despite the outsized break out, I am of the opinion that its dividend investment thesis remains extremely promising at forward yields of 6.05% compared to the sector median of 1.38%, despite paling in comparison to the 5Y mean of 12.33%.

This is especially since there remains great opportunities for higher, variable dividends in FQ1'26 and FQ2'26, attributed to SBLK's healthier balance sheet at a net debt to the annualized FQ4'25 adj EBITDA ratio of 1.13x (compared to a year ago at 2.01x, thanks to the ongoing debt repayment and the richer adj EBITDA).

Combined with the rather volatile Iran development and the likely to be prolonged path to full resolution, I am of the opinion that the dry bulkers' spot prices are likely to remain elevated in the intermediate term, aided by the projected growth in the dry bulk demand by +1.9% YoY in ton miles in FY2026 and the GDP growth forecast at +3.1% YoY in 2026.

As a result of the notably improved risk/reward well balancing the recent rally and the likely to be outsized FQ1'26/FQ2'26 performance metrics, I am upgrading the SBLK stock as a Buy.

data.title: 3D Systems: Aerospace And Defense Narrative Isn't Compelling
data.description:
3D Systems Corporation' (DDD) business continues to struggle, with system sales an area of particular weakness. While some of this can likely be attributed to company-specific issues, 3D Systems' peers are also facing similar issues. Aerospace and defense represent a material opportunity, although 3D Systems' recent growth in this area has been fairly modest. The company's margins also remain poor, despite ongoing cost-cutting efforts.

I previously suggested that green shoots in select markets are insufficient to offset broader structural and financial headwinds. Weakness is largely due to 3D Systems' dental business, along with a soft capital spend environment. This has been exacerbated by 3D Systems' relatively large exposure to the dental aligner market.

While the demand environment appears to be improving, I am skeptical that this will benefit 3D Systems in the short term. As a result, I remain "Neutral" on the stock, despite 3D Systems' seemingly attractive valuation.

Market Conditions

While soft economic conditions and rising geopolitical tensions have contributed to weak system sales for most additive manufacturing vendors, manufacturing survey data points towards a modest improvement in the demand environment. It is unclear to what extent this will lead to increased spending, though, particularly on more niche areas like additive manufacturing.

Aerospace and defense are clearly the strongest end markets currently, and most additive manufacturing companies are aggressively pursuing these opportunities. 3D Systems, along with other U.S. vendors, could benefit from the National Defense Authorization Act, which restricts the sourcing of 3D printing systems for DoD programs from foreign vendors. Aerospace and defense are likely to remain areas of strength going forward, but it is unclear whether 3D Systems' exposure is sufficient to meaningfully alter its trajectory.

Guidance and job openings don't point towards a meaningful growth acceleration in 2026 at the moment. This is not necessarily that surprising given 3D Systems' outsized exposure to the dental market. While Align Technologies (ALGN) has returned to modest growth, the improvement is probably insufficient to meaningfully alter 3D Systems' current trajectory.

3D Systems Business Updates

New product launches are driving 3D Systems' business at the moment. For example, the automotive business has been boosted by the introduction of the dual laser SLA 750 a little over 12 months ago. This is now being adopted in both motorsports and consumer automotive OEMs. Growth from the jewelry end market has been supported by the recent launch of a new wax printer (MJP 300W Plus), which reduces gold lost during the final polishing step. The new NextDent Jetted Denture platform is also beginning to drive sales.

Aerospace and defense, personalized health services, and dental are 3D Systems main target end markets. Aerospace and defense is the largest and one of the fastest-growing segments in 3D Systems' Industrial Solutions business. Revenue from these end markets only increased 16% in 2026, though. The manufacturing of metal parts (metal casting or direct metal printing) is the fastest-growing and highest-value portion of these markets, and 3D Systems has made significant investments in metal additive manufacturing in recent years. Satellite communications is another rapidly expanding market where the advantages of additive manufacturing are highly valued, and drones are likely to be another important application in coming years.

3D Systems' Personalized Healthcare business is being driven by craniomaxillofacial procedures and the company's partnership with Stryker (SYK). Personalized Health Services revenue increased by a double-digit percentage in 2025, with 3D Systems supporting over 18,000 personalized planning cases and manufacturing more than 260,000 implants in 2025.

The dental market also remains important for 3D Systems, and this is an area where the immediate outlook appears more mixed, even though demand for aligners is stabilizing. There is also the denture opportunity, which could grow significantly in coming years. There are roughly 180 million denture wearers globally, including 32 million in the United States, and aging populations globally are driving demand. While still currently somewhat niche, 3D Systems is confident that the majority of dental manufacturing will eventually transition to 3D printing. The company recently completed the full commercial release of the NextDent Jetted Denture Solution, and early customer feedback has been positive. This solution enables the production of single-piece, multi-material dentures with superior properties and opens up a market expected to be worth roughly $600 million by 2029.

3D Systems recently decided to pause investments in Systemic Bio. Systemic Bio was developing technology that could print vascularized human tissue on chips for use in drug testing. The company continues to invest in the development of 3D-printed human lungs in collaboration with United Therapeutics on the development of a 3D-printed human lung.

Financial Analysis

3D Systems generated $106.3 million in revenue in the fourth quarter, an increase of 3% YoY when adjusting for the Geomagic divestment. This growth is distorted by the fact that 3D Systems recorded a one-time medicine accounting adjustment in the fourth quarter of 2024 that reduced revenue by $8.7 million. When accounting for this adjustment, revenue was actually down 5% YoY in Q4. 3D Systems blamed the decline on weak printer and material demand. The company's Personalized Health Services business saw double-digit growth, though, and aerospace and defense revenue increased 16%. 3D Systems' dental business remains a weak spot, although this situation should improve as customer inventory levels stabilize.

3D Systems expects $91-$94 million in revenue in the first quarter of 2026, representing a decline of roughly 2% YoY at the midpoint. The company hasn't given full-year guidance, though, due to geopolitical uncertainty. 3D Systems expects 20% aerospace and defense growth in 2026, though, driven by the expansion of qualified applications into production. This guidance is soft relative to peers like Stratasys (SSYS), which likely stems from end market exposure.

3D Systems' gross margins remain low, which is due to a combination of soft sales and revenue mix. Despite this, operating profitability is improving, driven by cost-cutting measures. Non-GAAP operating expenses declined 23% YoY when adjusting for the Geomagic divestment. Cost reduction and efficiency programs have delivered around $55 million in annualized savings. Much of this appears to have been simplifying the company’s structure and eliminating excess overhead and capacity. 3D Systems expects a continued reduction in expenses through the first half of 2026 and is guiding to an adjusted EBITDA loss of $3-$5 million in the first quarter of 2026.

Conclusion

3D Systems' stock has had a strong start to the year, likely driven by optimism around the adoption of additive manufacturing in aerospace and defense applications. In addition, additive manufacturing costs have declined to the point that production use cases are becoming feasible. While this may seem like an attractive setup, much of 3D Systems' business is struggling. This situation is highlighted by the company's weak first-quarter guidance and lack of full-year guidance.

While 3D Systems' margins are likely to continue improving, I expect low growth to persist in the near term. 3D Systems' valuation is modest, meaning downside risk from current levels is relatively low. It isn't low enough to make a company with weak fundamentals appealing, though.

data.title: Cummins: A Return To Growth Solidifies The Bull Case
data.description:
The current year has unfolded pretty much like last year, with U.S. stock markets kicking off the new year on the backfoot before sputtering to life a couple of months down the line. Early last year, the markets suffered from tariff tantrums before U.S. President Donald Trump paused global tariffs in April; this year’s early uninspiring run was due to investors souring on the AI trade coupled with surging energy prices amid the war in Iran. However, the markets appear to have largely shrugged off the Middle East conflict on the belief that a resolution is not far off, though oil prices remain significantly elevated above pre-war levels. The S&P 500 is now comfortably in the green, along with 9 of the market’s 11 sectors. The U.S. economy continues to demonstrate a mix of mid-cycle resilience and late-cycle pressures, with solid GDP growth and strong corporate earnings counterbalanced by a cooling labor market and elevated inflation.

That probably explains why cyclical sectors of the economy like Industrials are still outperforming while defensive ones like Healthcare are underperforming. Indeed, nearly all industrial stocks I cover here on Seeking Alpha have netted double-digit returns in the year-to-date, led by specialty contractors MasTec (NYSE:MTZ) which has returned 71.0% YTD and MYR Group (NASDAQ:MYRG), up 52.0% YTD, while Power Solutions (NASDAQ:PSIX) has advanced 37.5% over the timeframe. This article is about Cummins Inc. (NYSE:CMI), Power Solutions’ bigger peer.

Similar to my first piece on PSIX nearly two years ago, CMI recently appeared on my radar due to the stock’s momentum, with a 14-Day RSI of 69.4 putting it on the edge of overbought territory. Cummins designs and manufactures diesel engines, natural gas engines, electric/hybrid powertrains and related components. The company also produces power systems including a wide array of electric generators, hydrogen engines and battery electric systems. Cummins' core truck engine business has been experiencing a prolonged cyclical downturn, with weakness in the North American on-highway commercial truck market negatively impacting engine segment volumes starting H2 2024.

Cummins operates through 5 key segments:

  • Engine--the company manufactures a wide range of diesel and natural gas-powered engines mainly for heavy-duty and medium-duty trucks. The Engine segment accounted for 26% of revenues in 2025 and 22% of EBITDA.

  • Components--Cummins manufactures a wide range of products that complement its Engine and Power Systems segments. The Components segment brought in 25% of Cummins full-year 2025 revenue and accounted for 22% of EBITDA.

  • Distribution--this is the company’s primary sales and support channel. Distribution is Cummins’ largest segment, accounting for 30% of 2025 revenue and 29% of EBITDA.

  • Power Systems–this is Cummins’ key growth engine, as we shall see shortly. This segment is organized around three sub-segments and product lines: Power Generation, Industrial and Generator Technologies. This pivotal segment accounted for 18% of revenue but a considerably bigger slice of EBITDA at 27%. This implies this segment has better margins than the core Engine segment.

  • Accelera– this burgeoning segment is Cummins’ zero-emissions business that mainly involves hydrogen production technologies. The segment brought in ~1% of 2025 and revenues and remains in the red with the company saying it's ''still in the early-stages of commercializing these technologies.''

Cummins’ business model exhibits a high level of vertical integration, with the company capable of providing complete, integrated powertrain solutions mainly thanks to acquisitions such as Meritor (axles/drivelines) as well as through joint ventures like Eaton Cummins (automated transmissions). This enables the company to achieve higher efficiency through systems optimization, and also drives a higher-than-average ROE for the company. Indeed, Cummins’ Return on Equity reading of 25.1% is more than double the sector median at 12.4 ,and also leads component and powertrain specialists like BorgWarner (NYSE:BWA) with 5.1%, Dana Incorporated (NYSE:DAN) with -6.4% and Traton SE (OTCPK:TRATF) with 8.5%. However, it trails Indiana-based automatic transmission specialist Allison Transmission (NYSE:ALSN) with 35.4% and even Power Solutions’ 93.5% thanks to the latter’s explosive revenue growth and margin expansion since 2024.

As noted above, Cummins’ topline growth has taken a hit in recent years. The company reported 2025 full-year revenues of $33.7 billion, good for 1% decrease with sales in North America declining 3% to $20.2 billion while international sales increased 2%Y/Y to $13.5 billion. Full year GAAP net income fell 28.3% to $2.8 billion, partly due to weakness in its truck engine business as well as $458 million in impairment and restructuring charges related to its hydrogen electrolyzer business within Accelera.

The Engine segment posted full-year revenue of $10.9 billion, good for a 7% Y/Y decline while Components revenue fell 13% to $10.1 billion due to the ongoing weakness in its legacy truck business. Both segments posted sharp declines in profitability, with EBITDA for the Engine business falling 16% Y/Y to $1.7 billion while the Components segment saw EBITDA contract 12% Y/Y to $1.6 billion. Cummins was merely a victim of a weak market for commercial trucks, with total medium- and heavy-duty sales in North America dropping 13.6% Y/Y to 416,467 units, with tariffs and economic headwinds mainly to blame. North America accounts for roughly two-thirds of Cummins’ revenue.

Thankfully, the company’s other major segments continue to be in the pink of health, and are projected to pull the company out of its tailspin in the current year. The Power Systems segment posted revenue of $7.5 billion, good for an impressive 16% Y/Y growth while the Distribution segment brought in 12.4 billion, representing a 9% Y/Y increase. Meanwhile, the Accelera unit saw revenue increase 11% Y/Y to $460 million. Looking at the bottom-line, Power Systems posted robust EBITDA growth of 44% Y/Y to the tune of $1.7 billion while Distribution EBITDA jumped 31% Y/Y to $1.8 billion. Accelera EBITA fell 17%Y/Y to -$896 million, in large part due to the charges mentioned above.

Return To Growth

Cummins issued a healthy outlook for FY 2026, expecting revenue growth of 3% to 8% and EBITDA margin between 17.0% and 18.0%, representing a 150 bps increase in margin at the midpoint of the guidance range mainly driven by high demand for data center backup power. The company’s truck business continues to face plenty of uncertainty, with the Cummins projecting sales for heavy-duty trucks in North America at 220,000 to 240,000 units, good for 0-10% Y/Y increase while sales for medium-duty trucks are expected to clock in at 110,000-120,000 units, also flat to up 10% Y/Y. Accelera revenues are expected to come in at $300 million-$350 million, representing a nearly 30%Y/Y decline with a net loss of $325 million to $355 million.

Interestingly, Cummins is moving ahead with its mission to produce cleaner vehicles despite the Trump administration recently rolling back Obama-era emissions policies. Cummins will go ahead with its X10 engine, the latest model within its futuristic HELM platforms, with limited production expected in 2026 and full production set to begin in 2027. Launched in 2024, HELM (Higher Efficiency, Lower emissions, and Multiple fuels) platforms are a series of fuel-agnostic, low-emission engines designed around a common base engine architecture but optimized to run on various fuels including advanced diesel, natural gas and hydrogen. I think this is a smart bet that could prove beneficial if future administrations revert to stricter emissions standards.

As I mentioned above, Cummins' Power Systems has emerged as the company’s key growth driver, thanks in large part to surging demand for data center power generation both in the U.S. and across global markets. The company expects global power generation revenues to increase 10%-20% for FY 2026, marking another year of strong growth. This segment is highly profitable, with EBITDA margins increasing 430 bps Y/Y to 22.7% of sales in 2025. In my September article on MYR Group , I pointed out that the United States is in the early innings of an electricity supercyle. Here’s an excerpt from that piece:

Last year, electricity demand in the U.S. snapped a 14-year period of stagnation after clocking in at 180 TWh, good for a 3.0% Y/Y increase. That marked the fifth fastest growth clip in 25 years. According to the International Energy Agency (IEA), demand increased by 2.7% in the first half of the current year and is expected to remain robust in the coming years driven by the proliferation of AI data centers, high-tech manufacturing in semiconductor fabrication and battery production industries, and the electrification of the transport and heating sectors.’

In February, the International Energy Agency(IEA) forecast that global electricity demand will grow at 3.5% CAGR over the next decade driven by buildout of AI data centers and continued uptake of electric vehicles, as well as rising industrial use and air conditioning. In the United States, the AI boom is expected to do much of the heavy lifting, with data centers projected to account for up to 12% of electricity use in 2028 up from 4.4% in 2023. Companies like MYRG that build transmission and distribution networks are first in-line to benefit from the ongoing AI boom and power infrastructure buildout; however, surging demand for reliable energy to support AI infrastructure has also led to increased orders for diesel and natural gas generators, Cummins’ forte. Data centers primarily use diesel generators for backup due to their rapid start-up times, superior fuel efficiency and general reliability when used for extended operations. Cummins’ industrial-grade generators are widely considered top-tier, offering a balance between cost and power output. Cummins is a dominant player in the diesel generator market, projected to grow at ~6% CAGR through 2031 to hit $25.6 billion.

Ditching Hydrogen

Cummins will continue developing its H2-ICE hydrogen combustion engines, with plans to launch a 15-liter engine in 2027. However, I believe the company took the right step by scaling back its electrolyzer business. A few years ago, I was pretty bullish about the nascent hydrogen sector. Back then, I was betting that the global hydrogen economy was ready to take off largely due to falling costs, with projections that hydrogen production costs were set to decline by up to 50% over the current decade after falling by a similar magnitude in the 2015-2020 period, thus achieving cost parity with other low-carbon alternatives. However, I was hardly the only hydrogen bull around: not too long ago, UBS analyst Michele DellaVigna predicted that hydrogen production would grow into a $1 trillion global market. And, Europe was ready to go all-in: back in 2021, the continent set an ambitious target to consume 20 Mt/year of renewable hydrogen and install at least 62 GW of electrolyzers by 2030 as part of the European Green Deal to decarbonize heavy industries.

Unfortunately, hydrogen continues to be a hard sell: WoodMac reported in 2024 that only 8.3Mtpa of the total announced hydrogen capacity of 135 Mtpa globally had been contracted, with only a quarter of that having reached a definitive sales and purchase agreement. Hydrogen uptake in Europe has been equally sluggish, with the region only managing to install ~308 MW of electrolyzers by 2024, less than 1% of its 2030 target. We have witnessed numerous hydrogen project cancellations in recent years, especially by Europe’s oil majors including BP Plc (NYSE:BP), Shell Plc (NYSE:SHEL), Equinor ASA (NYSE:EQNR) and Iberdrola (OTCPK:IBDRY). Others include Air Products (NYSE:APD) which halted new spending on its $4.5-billion hydrogen-to-ammonia project in Louisiana citing rising costs while steel-maker ArcelorMittal S.A. (NYSE:MT) cancelled plans to convert two of its German steel plants to hydrogen-based production at a cost of nearly $3 billion, despite being offered $1.4 billion in subsidies by the German government.

There are several reasons why the hydrogen economy has stalled. First off, the heavily favored green hydrogen produced through water electrolysis using renewable energy remains about 3x more expensive than gray hydrogen produced from steam methane reforming of natural gas. Indeed, green hydrogen production costs of $4 to $6/kg (roughly $28–$42/MMBtu) is multiples higher than current U.S. natural gas price of ~$2.70/MMBtu or even Europe’s natural gas currently at €39 per megawatt hour (approx. $13.50/MMBtu). Second, the round-trip efficiency of producing hydrogen, transporting it and then converting it back into electricity ranges from 20-40%, significantly lower than ~60% typical of modern Combined Cycle Gas Turbine (CCGT) plants. Finally, financing hydrogen projects is an arduous task, with producers typically demanding 10- to 20-year offtake agreements, a risky proposition for buyers amid technological immaturity and complex regulatory landscapes.

Given this backdrop, it would not have been prudent for Cummins to continue burning so much cash in an endeavor whose future viability remains in question. The hydrogen business is expected to book a loss of up to $355 million in the current year, still quite high in my view. However, I'm optimistic that the cash burn by this segment will now be muted going forward.

First Quarter Earnings

Cummins is slated to report first quarter earnings on 5th May before the market opens. The company issued full-year FY 2026 guidance but did not provide Q1 2026 guidance; however, Zacks Investment Research forecast based on the consensus of 7 analysts expect the company to report first quarter of EPS of what $5.79, a decrease from diluted EPS of $5.96 reported for Q1 2024. However, Cummins has a solid earnings track-record, having managed to exceed Wall Street estimates for four consecutive quarters, often by a wide margin.

Additionally, Wall Street is growing more bullish about Cummins' outlook, and has issued 10 upwards revisions for full-year EPS compared to 9 downgrades while full-year revenue has received 13 upgrades vs. 4 downgrades.

Investors should pay close attention to the company's margin trends with expectations of meaningful expansion after scaling back the hydrogen business. The full divestiture of Cummins' Atmus Filtration Technologies business in 2024 positively impacted the company's margins despite an initial drop in sales volume for the Components segment. It's quite likely that the negative impact on the topline will be less noticeable this time around.

Valuation & Risks

CMI appears overvalued after a scorching rally that saw the shares rocket 133% over the past 52 weeks. However, I don’t think valuations are too stretched, with a P/E GAAP (FWD) of 24.0 vs. 22.6 for the sector median; EV/Sales (FWD) of 2.6 vs. 2.2 and EV/EBITDA (FWD) of 14.5 vs. 12.4. Even better, the company is expected to grow earnings in double-digits over the next four years, with Wall Street projecting EPS growth of 10.9% in FY 26; 19.6% in FY 27, 15.2% in FY 2 and 19.9% in FY 29. That’s significantly higher than the expected average earnings growth of 12% for S&P 500 companies in 2026 and 10% in 2027. The healthy bottomline growth by CMI is projected to help the P/E ratio to fall from 26.4 in 2025 to 14.4 in 2029, giving the shares some room to run. Additionally, CMI scores well in other metrics such as profitability, growth, and momentum, earning it a Hold rating on Seeking Alpha Quant.

An economic slowdown, or worse still, a recession, is probably the biggest risk facing CMI. Commercial trucks are mainly used to move goods and demand is closely tied to manufacturing, construction and overall economic growth. Businesses tend to cut back on purchasing new trucks when the economy is struggling, with U.S. truck sales crashing ~60% during the 2008 global financial crisis.

The company clearly details this key risk in its latest 10-K filing:

Our markets are cyclical in nature and we face periods when demand fluctuates significantly higher or lower than our normal operating levels, including variability driven by supply chain inconsistency. Accurately forecasting our expected volumes and appropriately adjusting our capacity are important factors in determining our results of operations and cash flows. We manage our capacity by adjusting our manufacturing workforce, capital expenditures and purchases from suppliers. In periods of weak demand, we may face under-utilized capacity and un-recovered overhead costs, while in periods of strong demand, we may experience unplanned costs and could fail to meet customer demand. We cannot guarantee that we will be able to adequately adjust our manufacturing capacity in response to significant changes in customer demand, which could harm our business.

However, the recent broad-based market rally appears to suggest that economic/recession worries have been put on the back-burner. On another brighter note, Cummins could benefit after Trump announced 25% tariffs on imported medium and heavy-duty trucks in October, which could force manufacturers to build more vehicles domestically using U.S.-made components.

Takeaway

Whereas investors are growing wary of the AI boom and attendant volatility, companies demonstrating tangible revenue gains from AI power infrastructure buildout such as CMI could stand out. Cummins’ Power Systems business is clearly compensating for ongoing uncertainties in the company’s legacy truck engine business, a welcome pivot because the ongoing electricity boom is a secular megatrend expected to last for years, if not decades. CMI shares look moderately overvalued, but can still outperform the market unless the U.S. economic outlook worsens considerably.

CMI's stretched valuation after a wild run could limit near-term gains for this stock. Nevertheless, I rate CMI a Buy for long-term shareholders.

data.title: Signify: Collecting Dividends While Riding Out Cyclical Headwinds
data.description:
Introduction

Signify (PHPPY) has been a pretty volatile stock in the past year as its share price has been known to lose 20% and gain 20% in relatively short periods of time. Subsequent to the publication of the 2025 results the share price came under severe pressure again as the bottom line result was not as good as anticipated. On top of that, it sounded like 2026 would be another difficult year for this leader in LED lighting. I originally invested in Signify based on its free cash flow profile rather than its bottom line earnings results and for now I'm sticking to my position in anticipation of a better performance in 2027.

The company's main listing (ticker symbol LIGHT) on Euronext Amsterdam is much more liquid, with approximately 1 million shares traded on a daily basis. I will use the Euro as base currency throughout this article.

The cash flows remain very strong, and that’s a key element for me

Before discussing my expectations for 2026 and 2027 I wanted to take a step back and use the 2025 results to explain how invite companies free cash flow result is stronger than its net income result. This is an integral part of my investment thesis.

Whereas the Consumer business grew everywhere except in China, the Professional division was growing in the USA but decreased in Europe. The real underlying issue was the OEM segment where volume reductions happened hand in hand with pricing pressure (resulting in Signify’s margins getting hammered). Meanwhile, the over the counter sales(and conventional sales) decreased as well, but this was generally expected. In fact, going forward, I am assuming the sale of conventional lighting will decrease towards zero in the next half decade as these orders are mainly to replace older lightbulbs in fixtures the owner doesn’t want to replace yet. Those fixtures will eventually all be replaced by LED lighting, reducing the need for conventional light ‘bulbs’.

The revenue decrease remained limited to approximately 6%, as the full-year revenue decreased to just under 5.8B EUR. The COGS decreased at a similar pace, resulting in a mid single digit decrease in the gross margin. Not great, but also not fatal.

But of course, a mid single digit impact on a gross margin results in a double digit impact on the operating income level despite seeing cost savings measures: the SG&A expenses decreased by about 4% while the company also reduced its R&D expenses by about 6%. But that wasn’t sufficient to compensate for the lower gross margin, and the reported operating income fell to 383M EUR. Fortunately the net finance expenses also decreased (61M EUR versus 82M EUR), but the damage had been done: the bottom line shows a net income of 259M EUR, of which 254M EUR was attributable to the shareholders of Signify. This resulted in an EPS of 2.06 EUR based on the weighted average share count of just over 123M shares. Keep in mind the net share count has dropped below 120M shares, so if you’d calculate the net profit based on the net share count as of the end of 2025, you would end up with an EPS of just over 2 EUR.

Moving over to the cash flow result, the reported operating cash flow was 531M EUR. That being said, I’m deducting 32M EUR in working capital contributions as well as the 17M EUR difference in taxes owed and taxes paid. On top of that, I need to deduct approximately 65M EUR in lease payments as well. After taking all these elements into consideration, the adjusted operating cash flow was approximately 417M EUR.

As you can see above under the tab ‘cash flows from investing activities’, the total net capex was approximately 91M EUR (higher than in FY 2024, so we can’t really accuse the company of underinvesting), resulting in a net underlying free cash flow result of just over 2.7 EUR per share. The dividend increased by a symbolic cent to 1.57 EUR per share, which means it increases at a slower rate than the repurchase of the stock happened.

The 1.57 EUR will cost Signify just under 190M EUR in cash based on the current share count and that is just around 60% of the 2025 underlying FCF. This indeed means the 8% dividend remains well covered, both by the EPS and by the FCFPS. The stock will go ex-dividend at the end of this month with a payment date in the first half of May.

Resetting my expectations for 2026 and 2027

While the fully covered dividend remains attractive, I also shouldn’t be blind to the new reality. The 345M EUR in anticipated FCF in 2026 is now unlikely to happen (as I was anticipating a 1-2% revenue decrease this year, but we’ll likely have to look at a high single digit decrease unless things start to pick up towards the end of the year).

It’s perhaps more prudent to assume a 10-15% YoY decrease in 2026, before the impact of the cost savings program (hopefully) kicks in in 2027.

The company will open its books for the first quarter at the end of this week, and there are a few elements I will be focusing on:

First of all, I want to see and hear additional management comments on the situation in the construction sector. Signify’s management referred to a slowdown in this segment to justify the weaker than expected 2025 results, so I’d like to hear if this segment is picking up and if there are any geographical outliers (regions where growth is picking up faster than elsewhere).

Secondly, and somewhat related to that, I want to hear if my anticipation of a 10-15% revenue decrease this year is a somewhat realistic target, or if the situation could be worse.

Signify is one of the few companies that actually publishes the consensus estimates ahead of the quarter on its website, and looking at the summary, the average and median net free cash flow is still 353-367M EUR for this year. That’s better than I am expecting (as I now think my 345M EUR estimate may be too optimistic). This may be explained by including working capital contributions in the consensus estimates while I calculate the free cash flow excluding changes in the working capital. The average and median EPS for 2026 is 1.28 EUR and 1.24 EUR respectively for this year.

Moving to 2027, the expected average free cash flow drops by in excess of 10% to 313M EUR (which is more in line with my expectations), strengthening my suspicion the 2026 consensus will include a contribution from changes in the working capital position.

Investment thesis

Unfortunately Signify has a history of missing expectations and barely exceeds and beats those expectations or guidance. That's why I wouldn't be surprised to see another miss which could result in the share price coming under pressure once again.

I currently still have a long position in the stock which I now consider to be a high yield investment but since the April expiration date last week I have no written put options anymore. I definitely wouldn't mind too benefits from the increase volatility levels (which pushes option premiums higher) both I have no intention to sell any put options before the Q1 results are out. Additionally, the stock will go ex-dividend at the end of this month, So I may wait for the coupon to be detached before selling put options.

And just to answer my own question in the title. I think there is value in Signify, but it is a cyclical business. I have purchased the stock to be positioned for an improvement in the demand and a margin recovery. But exactly because it is a cyclical stock, I also realize this is not a ‘buy and hold forever’ type of investment. For now, the 8% dividend yield is attractive and a good compensation for waiting for the situation to improve. But I would like to see the first signs of an improvement in the next 12-18 months.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Driehaus Small/Mid Cap Growth Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The March quarter started off well as January and February were strong opening months for the U.S. equity market. Key drivers of the market were favorable, including: strong economic growth, robust earnings (especially for small caps), the Federal Reserve's rate cutting cycle, tax cuts (as the so called Big Beautiful Bill became effective in 2026), expanded deregulation, the ISM PMI Manufacturing index entering expansion territory (above 50), and inflation gradually easing along with long-term interest rates. These positives outweighed negatives such as the stalled labor market and growing concerns about private credit. As a result, through February, equities were up for the year, market breadth expanded and small caps outperformed large caps. Then on February 28th, the US & Israel launched strikes on Iran, initiating the Iran War. This was in response to growing potential threats by the Iranian regime and the recent brutal attacks on its own people.

The Iran War

The Iran War has dramatically changed the equity market environment for the time being. Equities fell sharply in the month of March as the price of crude oil nearly doubled, interest rates rose, the US dollar rallied, economic uncertainty spiked and investor sentiment plunged. WTI crude oil increased from $65 two days before the war to over $100 by the end of March just over a month later. Other key global crude oil benchmarks, such as, Brent, Oman, and Dubai were even higher. On most individual trading days in March, the price of crude oil was the critical factor. Favorable war headlines caused the price of crude oil to fall and equities to rally in an inverse relationship. However, the opposite was true on most days, where negative war headlines and higher crude prices caused equity multiples to compress. Overall, the fog of war remains thick.

As of one week into April, the US and Iran have agreed to a two-week ceasefire. The ceasefire is a clear positive that for the time being removing the threat of a highly escalatory bombing campaign on Iran's power and economic infrastructure and avoids ground troops and other dire outcomes. It also creates a window for the resumption of ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz (SoH) albeit under Iran's new exorbitant toll system, potentially providing relief from the global energy shock. However, the ceasefire is fragile. Trust between US and Iran is low. The peace plans put forth by both sides have several demands that seem unrealistic. However, they are a starting point for continued negotiations. The SoH is the most important factor for crude oil, the global economy and the equity market and it remains mostly closed. The US says the ceasefire's durability hinges on the Strait reopening fully. A failure in talks could see a rapid resumption of hostilities, but successful talks could de-escalate the broader crisis and would be a major positive for the equity market.

Trump's decision to accept the ceasefire and push out his most recent deadline again is a reflection of the limits of his own appetite for economic and political damage through further escalation rather than substantive progress on a nuclear deal, regional proxies, sanctions and real regime change, key issues which remain extremely difficult to resolve. Nonetheless, for the markets and the global economy, the ceasefire and negotiations are a major positive. It is more likely than not that the fragile two-week ceasefire will be extended longer term, though there are many conflicting headlines that will likely continue to create severe daily market volatility. For instance, Iran has continued to bomb multiple middle east countries after the ceasefire started and Iran is objecting to Israel's escalating attacks on Lebanon, but it does appear the US and Iran have stopped attacking each other.

Before this ceasefire, Trump dramatically increased his threats on the Iranian Regime and the IRGC (Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corp) demanding they open the Strait. His ultimatum to open the Strait or face attacks on Iran's power plants, bridges and other key infrastructure grew louder and louder. These threats likely have contributed to the tentative ceasefire agreement. It had been our base case that one way or another Trump would declare victory relatively soon and that would cause the equity market to recover as the US economy should continue to grow albeit with higher inflation due to energy prices in the near term.

The Strait of Hormuz remains a critical bottleneck, both in terms of US-Iran negotiations and for the global economy. If it can fully re-open, its impact on global crude oil prices and supply will lessen. Outside of the Strait, energy infrastructure has been severely damaged by Iran in key Persian Gulf countries disrupting the supply of crude oil, natural gas, aluminum and key petrochemicals. Even with a re-opened Strait, the supply of these commodities will be impacted for months and potentially years as it will take time to repair production facilities, refineries, and other key infrastructure.

Other Key Macro Considerations

Artificial Intelligence (AI) continues to be the major theme driving the performance of multiple parts of the market. The market has separated many companies within various industries into AI beneficiaries or those disrupted by AI. For instance, many tech hardware companies and industrial companies are benefitting from the AI capex cycle which is fueling data center growth as well as the utility capex cycle driving an upgrade and expansion of the US electrical grid. On the other hand, software, IT services, business services and many other digital or white-collar businesses are being viewed by the market as disrupted by AI. Overall, AI compute demand continues to exceed supply which gives confidence to the sustainability of the hyperscaler capex data center buildout.

Outside of AI, many cyclical groups are performing well within industrials, materials, energy, transports, and semiconductors. This is a function of various favorable economic trends. The ISM PMI manufacturing index posted its third consecutive month above 50 (expansionary), which comes after a prolonged period of contraction. We view this expansion as likely sustainable given signs of strong demand in multiple industries and themes, as well as a general low level of inventory across the economy. Together this should drive further production to meet demand and replenish inventory stocks. There are also positive signs of improving demand via accelerating new order growth in the PMI reports (the strongest since early 2023), as well as recent strength in trucking, bank loan, and construction data.

Key risks beyond crude oil and the war do remain. One is the growing stress in the private credit markets. Equity investors have had growing concerns about cracks in these opaque private finance markets. Blue Owl Capital, Inc. has been at the epicenter of these concerns as it and others have seen elevated requests for withdrawals from their funds, especially from retail investors. Credit crises often start out as small cracks and then grow to be larger than was initially expected. So, the market is closely watching for signs of contagion, and potential signs of systematic problems impacting banks, insurance companies, business development companies, and investment banks. A key question will be, has the weakness in the software industry or the huge expansion in the data center market had a negative impact on private credit. This upcoming earnings season will be critical to monitor to see if there are signs of further stress in private credit within the banking sector.

Another concern is the labor market. For an otherwise growing economy, job creation is unusually weak, though it was stronger than expected in the month of March. Healthcare and construction are the only major parts of the economy creating new jobs. There is a shortage of skilled, blue-collar labor but there are signs of excess supply in knowledge based or white-collar jobs particularly in technology, finance and among recent college graduates. The fear is that AI is beginning to negatively impact job creation. For those unemployed it is taking longer to find a job. Overall, initial jobless claims are still low historically, but so is new job creation. The result is a still tight but very stagnant labor market. The unemployment rate of 4.3% is low but there are risks the labor market will weaken further, which is driving concerns about the so-called K-shaped economy.

Valuations remain appealing for small caps, especially compared to large caps. The investment case for small-cap stocks is compelling when looking at small cap earnings, which have been robust and accelerating since the June quarter of last year. Also, small-cap earnings are expected to outgrow large cap earnings for the next few quarters as shown in the bar chart below. History further shows that small caps typically outperform during the first five years or more off a new market cycle, which we believe began a year ago on April 9th, 2025, when the market bottomed post Liberation Day. Additionally, small caps and equities overall consistently do well for at least the 12 months following the beginning of a rate-cutting cycle by the Fed.

Exhibit 1: S&P 600 vs. S&P 500 Equal-Weight Y/Y Pct. Change In EPS

Source: Strategas, Bloomberg

Performance Review

The Driehaus Small/Mid Cap Growth (DSMDX) strategy outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2500 Growth Index, by approximately 450 basis points for the first quarter of 2026. The strategy gained 0.98% versus a decline of 3.52% for the Russell 2500 Growth, a gain of 2.04% for the Russell 2500, and a decline of 4.60% for the S&P 500.1 From a sector perspective, the top three contributing sectors on a relative basis were technology, industrials, and energy while the bottom three sectors on a relative basis were healthcare, consumer discretionary, and financials.

1 The performance data represents the strategy's composite of small/mid cap growth accounts managed by Driehaus Capital Management LLC (DCM). These returns are estimated for the period as the underlying accounts' data is yet to be reconciled to the custodian bank. Net of fee returns reflect the payment of advisory fees and in some instances, other fees and expenses such as administrative and custodian fees while the gross of fee returns do not. Both are net of brokerage commissions charged to the accounts and reflect the reinvestment of income and other earnings. The performance data shown above represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

Sector Attribution

From a sector perspective, the top two contributing sectors on a relative basis for the quarter were information technology and industrials. The health care and consumer discretionary sectors performed negatively on a relative basis for the quarter

Exhibit 2: Sector Attribution Relative to Benchmark

Source: Driehaus. Note: The sector attribution relative to benchmark for each MSCI/GICS Sector is equal to the sum of the individual Attribution Effects for that MSCI/GICS Sector. The cash weighting at 3/31/26 was 4.5%.

Sector Commentary – Contributors:

Information Technology

Information technology was the largest relative sector outperformer and the second largest gainer in absolute terms. Our technology holdings gained 10.5% versus a decline of 10.95% for the benchmark. Strong stock selection was the primary driver of outperformance along with being overweight strong sub-industries such as semiconductors and communications equipment as well as having near zero exposure to out of favor areas such as enterprise software and IT services.

Tech hardware holdings related to semiconductors, semi cap equipment, optical equipment, and other data center infrastructure areas performed well as they are seeing strong earnings due to improving demand and rising AI-related capital spending and data center growth. Specifically, two optical component suppliers and a semi cap equipment company were the top individual contributors as they reported strong earnings driven by growing AI data center buildouts. Overall, the quarter's performance and positioning favor hardware companies benefiting from AI and the avoidance of software and IT service companies that are under pressure as they are potentially being disrupted by competitive AI threats. The strategy slightly increased exposure to the tech sector during the quarter but maintains an underweight exposure overall.

Industrials

Industrials was the second largest contributor on a relative basis and the largest on an absolute basis. Our industrial holdings gained 9.1% versus 8.0% for the benchmark. The strategy increased exposure to the sector during the quarter and continued to maintain an overweight position. Outperformance was driven by strong earnings and improving outlooks across a variety of industries including commercial aerospace, defense, satellite services, machinery, engineering & construction and other AI infrastructure and electrical grid related holdings. An engineering & construction company focused on the electrical grid was the top performer as it reported strong backlog growth, accelerating revenue growth and better than expected earnings. The second-best performer was a commercial aerospace company that saw strength due to a new product driving its addressable market expansion as well as raised guidance on its earnings report.

Energy

Energy performed well as it generated the third largest outperformance on a relative basis and absolute basis. Outperformance was due to being overweight the sector as our energy holdings gained 24.9% versus 26.2% for the benchmark. Energy was the market's strongest sector during the quarter as the Iran War dramatically impacted crude oil and other commodity prices as supply concerns grew due to the damage inflicted on Persian Gulf energy infrastructure and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, blocking 20% of the world's crude oil from reaching global markets. The portfolio saw strong gains in holdings in oilfield services, uranium, and exploration & production. An offshore oil services company and a uranium mining company were the top contributors as both reported strong earnings, favorable industry supply-demand conditions, and a favorable outlook driving performance.

Sector Commentary – Detractors:

Healthcare

Healthcare was the largest detractor on a relative basis and on an absolute basis. Our healthcare holdings declined 8.5% versus a decline of 5.8% for the benchmark. Performance was impacted by negative stock performance in biotech and pharma and medical devices. We reduced sector exposure during the quarter, finishing March underweight the sector.

Consumer Discretionary

Consumer discretionary was the second largest detractor on a relative basis and the third largest in absolute terms. Our consumer discretionary holdings declined 12.8% versus a decline of 6.6% for the benchmark. Performance was impacted by negative stock performance results across specialty retail, leisure products, and a couple restaurant holdings as macro concerns weighed on sentiment. We reduced sector exposure during the quarter, finishing March underweight versus the benchmark.

Financials

Financials was another large sector detractor on a relative basis and second largest on an absolute basis. Our financial holdings declined 29.1% versus a decline of 12.2% for the benchmark. While the strategy's large sector underweight provided some favorable offset, stock underperformance remained the primary headwind as weak performance occurred across the sector, including in specialty finance, payments, capital markets, and insurance holdings. Sector weakness was broad based due to macro concerns such as the Iran War, private credit concerns and rising interest rates. We reduced our sector exposure further during the quarter.

Strategy Overview and Positioning

In terms of portfolio positioning, by sector, industrials remained our largest absolute weight, followed by health care, and technology. On a relative basis, the strategy is overweight industrials, energy, and materials while the strategy is underweight financials, healthcare, consumer discretionary, and information technology. The biggest sector changes in terms of relative weight were increases in technology, industrials, materials, and energy and decreases in health care, consumer discretionary, and financials.

Exhibit 3: Variation in Ending Weight

Source: Driehaus. Note: Change in relative weight is the difference between the change in ending weight from the previous quarter. The cash weighting at 3/31/26 was 4.5%.

Market Outlook

Overall, the macro data suggests the economy is resilient and continues to grow nicely. As mentioned above there are signs of strength across multiple industries and themes but there are risks surrounding the war, crude oil prices, private credit, and the labor market. The outlook for further Fed rate cuts is complicated by these risks, but investors are still expecting additional cuts this year. There will be a new Fed Chair soon when the new nominee Kevin Warsh is confirmed. The market will have to adjust to Warsh and historically the market consistently tests a new Fed Chair resulting in a market correction or some market stress early in a new Chair's term. However, Warsh is experienced, very qualified and he should be another in a long line of strong, capable leaders of the FOMC.

We believe if the fog of war begins to clear and the new tenuous ceasefire agreement proves sustainable the impact on the markets will be very positive, though the damaged energy infrastructure in the Middle East will impact the supply of energy products globally and the Strait of Hormuz needs to be re-opened soon. In sum, if peace prevails, the market will likely recover strongly.

In terms of portfolio positioning, we have an attractive mix of secular and cyclical growth holdings. By sector, industrials is our largest absolute weight, followed by healthcare, technology, energy, and consumer discretionary. On a relative basis, the strategy is overweight industrials, energy, and materials. The strategy is underweight healthcare, technology, consumer discretionary and financials.

Active share represents the share of portfolio holdings that differ from the benchmark index holdings. Beta is a measure of a portfolio's volatility. A beta of 1.00 implies perfect historical correlation of movement with the market. A higher beta manager will rise and fall more rapidly than the market, whereas a lower beta manager will rise and fall slower. Information Ratio (IR) measures a portfolio manager's ability to generate excess returns relative to a benchmark, but also attempts to identify the consistency of the investor. This ratio will identify if a manager has beaten the benchmark by a lot in a few months or a little every month. The higher the IR the more consistent a manager is and consistency is an ideal trait. For fixed-income securities, the benchmark is the T-bill. For equities, the benchmark is the S&P 500. data.title: Purpose-Built Humanoids For Specific Jobs
data.description:
By Rafael Silva

In September of last year, following the release of the World Robotics Report by the International Federation of Robotics (IFR), a panel discussed the feasibility of humanoids entering homes in the not-too-distant future. The consensus at the time was that the current generation of humanoid robots was neither safe, dexterous, nor cheap enough to be useful within a household.

During that discussion, a concept emerged that is now playing out across the industry in real time. An ultimate, all-purpose humanoid that can seamlessly transition from welding car frames to folding laundry would no doubt be revolutionary. But something more logical is becoming apparent as we move deeper into the current robotics supercycle. There will likely be different humanoids for different tasks. After all, we’re leaving behind the days when developing a single robot would take enormous resources. Why should one robot be able to do everything?

The recent contrast between Boston Dynamics’ Atlas and Fauna Robotics’ (recently acquired by Amazon) Sprout hints at what the future may look like. In that future, humanoids won’t be defined by a single form factor. Instead, they will be defined by specialized platforms built for specific tasks. After all, no one expects their washing machine to also mow the lawn.

The Purpose-Built Factory Worker: Atlas

On the industrial side, we have Boston Dynamics' Atlas. Speaking after its launch, the design and engineering team were keen to explain that the latest Atlas is “a departure from the typical humanoid look, favoring industrial utility over human likeness.” With custom actuators two to three times stronger than off-the-shelf options, passive heat distribution to eliminate failure-prone fans in the limbs, and a 110-pound instant payload capacity, Atlas is built strictly for the factory floor. It looks like a machine because it has to act and work like one.

Atlas Prioritizes Industrial Utility Over Human Likeness

While much was made about purposefully abandoning the traditional, approachable aesthetic in favor of rugged utility, Atlas still very much looks like a friendly humanoid, complete with a cute round face, rather than the pure industrial machine the Boston Dynamics team tried to paint. Still, the team chose to highlight the shift in appearance. Perhaps this points to their recognition of a market ready to invest, with a focus on how these machines can work, maintain uptime (repairability), and deliver on ROI. In short, they see their clients moving away from questions about feasibility or appetite to questions about implementation. This is a significant step from where the industry was a year ago.

The Approachable Consumer Platform: Sprout

On the opposite end of the spectrum is Sprout. In late March 2026, Amazon accelerated its push into consumer robotics by acquiring New York-based startup Fauna Robotics. Their flagship bipedal humanoid is everything Atlas is not.

Sprout’s Soft-Touch Design Targets Human Interaction

Standing just 42 inches tall and weighing only 50 pounds, Sprout isn’t going to carry heavy payloads or weld automotive parts. Instead, it features a soft-touch exterior, expressive capabilities, and an approachable design engineered specifically for human interaction in homes, schools, and social spaces. Currently positioned as a developer platform, Sprout runs on an embedded Nvidia Jetson Orin computer. It is designed to form memories and engage naturally with people.

It must be said that Sprout is overall still in early development and commercialization stages. It’s unlikely to enter anyone’s home anytime soon. But Fauna, a company founded in 2024, has been able to develop a unique platform capable of serving a market that the big players had not been prioritizing. That speaks volumes about the fluidity of the robotic scene today.

In acquiring Fauna, Amazon is betting that the domestic and service sectors require a fundamentally different machine, one where approachability, software integration, and safety around humans (and pets!) matter far more than raw torque.

Different Tasks, Different Bots: The ROBO Advantage

This divergence is a good one for the robotics ecosystem. Just as the automotive industry relies on both heavy-duty trucks and compact commuter cars, the automated economy will likely require industrial humanoids, domestic humanoids, and many subsets within those categories. Trying to build one robot to do all creates a “jack of all trades, master of none” scenario.

For the ROBO Global Robotics and Automation Index (ROBO), this specialization presents a major tailwind. It expands the total addressable market (TAM) by opening new, currently unserved verticals. Meanwhile, it multiplies the demand for core components. The strategy covers both of these.

A key advantage of the ROBO strategy is that, because it is guided by a board of strategic advisors made up of world-renowned robotics pioneers and industry experts, it is uniquely positioned to evaluate the entire humanoid ecosystem. This means the index goes beyond just holding big names. It allows the strategy to actively identify and capture the developers bringing the emerging humanoids to life, as well as the “picks and shovels” companies making the components and technologies that are enabling the rapid expansion of humanoids.

By combining the component makers with the end-product creators, the strategy exposes investors to the full spectrum of the humanoid expansion, regardless of whether the factory floor or the living room scales first.

ROBO is the underlying index for the ROBO Global Robotics & Automation ETF (ROBO), the L&G ROBO Global Robotics and Automation UCITS ETF (ROBO.LN), and the Global X ROBO Global Robotics & Automation ETF (ROBO.AU).

Investment Process

We seek to invest in high-quality, undervalued companies with strong balance sheets and shareholder-oriented management teams.

Undervaluation

Determining the intrinsic value of a business is the heart of our research process. Intrinsic value represents the amount that a buyer would pay to own a company's future cash flows. We seek to invest at a significant discount to our estimate of the intrinsic value of a business.

Business Quality

We seek to invest in companies with histories of generating strong free cash flow, improving returns on capital and strong competitive positions in their industries.

Financial Strength

We believe that investing in companies with strong balance sheets helps to reduce the potential for capital risk and provides company management the ability to build value when attractive opportunities are available.

Shareholder-Oriented Management
